Upcoming Events

COC Club Meeting on March 8

COC is hosting a club meeting at the Coco's in Lake Forest Park on March 8 and all are welcome. There will be an informal, buy-your-own dinner, from 5pm to 6pm. Mike and Vivien Fritz will take us cross-county in their Airstream with their 8 minute video and add some commentary along the way. We will then have a "quick O tip". At 6:30 we shift to the COC board meeting proper. All are welcome to stay, but you are also most welcome to just come for the social aspects. (Detail about the trail work projects at Woodland and St. Edward State Parks and other breaking news about the US Champs are on the agenda. So is a brief discussion on OCAD 5 vs. 6, which route to take.)

POTLUCK

March 15, 1998 Ingemar Jansson Haverstad from Norway will be in Seattle in March. Ingemar was a student at the University of Washington. While here, he was a member of Cascade Orienteering Club. You may have seen his name on the University of Washington map. (At that time he was not yet a Haverstad.)

We are having a potluck for those who wish to come and say hi to Ingemar. The potluck will be at Robert & Sandy Kish Millers, 6003 Greenwood Ave. N, Seattle. Time will be 3 - 7 p.m. If you knew Ingemar or wish to meet Ingemar, please join us. Call 206-782-1437 with any questions.

WIOL and WINTER SERIES

I would like to add my thanks to the folks that made the WIOL school league and Winter Series possible. I will not name names for fear of leaving someone out. I just want to let the workers know that I appreciate the time commitment it took to make these events successes. Sandy Kish Miller

News and Notes

Woodland Park Trail Project

COC will be hosting a trail project in Woodland Park this late April or early May. We will have all the details on the Howard's PNW website in short order. The club will also return to St. Ed later in the season for another project. Of course, anyone is welcome to pitch in; matter-of-fact, we would really like your help in staying in good graces with the land mangers and the public.

Woodland Park staff are really excited about us coming and helping out with some much needed trail work. This sounds like a truly win-win situation for us and them. Hope you will consider joining us for the day in late April or early May. For more info, please call Rick H at 206.367.2606.

Orienteering in the News

Eileen Breseman, COC Publicity and Promotion

New connections for publicity are widening our exposure to the public.

On the Internet: We have our event calendar listed, a writeup about what orienteering is all about, as well as a direct connection to the Pacific NW Orienteering Website at two new sites. Try these: www.ontherun.com a runner's guide to what's hap-

[Page 2]

pening in the Northwest; www.sports-etc.com a monthly multi-sport magazine in its 10th year; http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html our own Pacific NW O website created and continually updated by Howard Halter, webmaster extraordinaire. Great information and a link to the US Championships coming in August with on-line entry application.

Radio: KMTT The Mountain station deejay offered to make public service announcements about us during his shift between 6-10pm Monday through Friday.

Newspaper: For those who did not get the word, The Seattle Times covered us both at St. Edward State Park in December, and at Lynndale Park on the trim course with member Jim Siscel, and gave us a really nice writeup and large photo in the January 9th pullout, Weekend Happenings (centerfold!) of The Seattle Times.

Future plans: We plan to make an easy set-up informational reader board at meets for onsite education, publicity, and information. We hope also to connect with local neighborhood papers to include upcoming meets in their area. If you have additional ideas they would be welcomed. Contact Eileen Breseman breseman@msn.com or (425) 483-6592. Publicity gets the people out to our meets!

It would be very appreciated if you - yes, you - took a few moments to give these supportive media people some positive feedback on their coverage of our orienteering doings. It goes a long way on the goodwill-o-meter.

What's Happening in SACO Land?

Frank Skorina, SACO

SACO land is Southeastern Washington, i.e, Tri-Cities and Walla Walla. You may have heard of our free Score-O meet on January 31 at Howard Amon in Richland (results elsewhere in this issue) followed by our annual meeting. But there has been a few more developments this year.

On Feb. 13, I held a seminar for 29 at the YMCA in Walla Walla. I gave a short introduction, showed a video, and made a little meet in the big gym on a map that I made the day before. YMCA Program Director Tom Kelleher remarked, "Cooooooool!" I do not think this will be the last time I have heard from Tom.

We have also moved on with some mapping projects. Ivar completed the first OCAD draft of my entire Pioneer Park (Walla Walla) field notes and I have already sent him back some corrections. Hey, we are amateurs at this so it will take a few passes.

Ivar and I also spent a day at Camp Wooten up on the Tucannon River east of Dayton. We used a laser range finder, compasses, and paces to get data for a base map of the area. Our motivation? I have held seminars for over 300 kids there on a primitive park map. I will be having contact with a few hundred more this year.

Our Spring schedule is as follows

Sun, Mar 29 Sacajawea; Sat, Apr 11 Bennington; Sun, Apr 26 Horn Rapids; Sun, May 10 Rooks Park; and Sat, May 23 Wallula Junction

We have obtained permission for all but Wallula Junction, although I do not anticipate much trouble because it is presently U.S. Army Corps of Engineer land as is Mill Creek Project (Bennington/Rooks Park). (There is some talk about the Umatilla tribes leasing the lands, but it will remain open to the public.)

Other happenings

Fri, Mar 13 Walla Walla Outdoor Adventure fair at the YMCA featuring North Pole soloist Helen Thayer. We will have one of about 20 booths and an average of 7 seconds to sell the sport. Any ideas?

Thu, Apr 09 Whitman Seminar for Outdoor Education class.

Thu, Apr 23 Camp Wooten for 110 6th graders from Dayton, Touchet, and Starbuck.

Fri-Sun, May 8-10 Walla Walla Balloon Stampede with meet at Rooks Park meet on Sunday. Scores of hotair balloons, crafts, booths, and more. I can even find you a place to stay.

Hope to see you soon!

Sacajawea Annual Report

Ivar Husa with input from Frank Skorina

This was an exceptionally active year. 417 people had contact with the club, through a combination of eight regular events (148 participants) and ten special sessions (269 participants) for Boy Scout troops, church groups, a local college, and grade schools.

Three new areas were mapped this year. Pioneer Park West (1:3000 scale. 0.1 sq km) was mapped in the city of Walla Walla and Howard Amon Park (1:4000 scale, 0.15 sq km) in the city of Richland. The small maps, in populated areas will facilitate our teaching of young people. The third area mapped is near Wallula, WA, at the confluence of the Columbia and Walla Walla Rivers (15 miles south of the confluence of the Columbia and Snake Rivers). Two maps have been created from this area. The Madame Dorion map (1:5000, 0.3 sq km) centers about a park with facilities, and is suitable for beginner and perhaps some intermediate courses. This map is contained within the much larger Wallula Junction map (1:15000, 10 sq km), which is fully 7 km from one end to the other.

The club gratefully acknowledges their first corporate sponsor, Sporthaus, which donated the use of a $250 laser range finder for map making.

Orienteering Hotline!

Rick Hood, COC

Richard Staudt has generously agreed to become the COC Hotline spokesperson/editor. Thank you Richard!!!

Richard at some point may adjust the outgoing message format, which for those not familiar with the system is as follows:

[Page 3]

When one dials the Hotline (206-783-3866) one hears the main message. This message provides general information of interest to anyone wishing to try O or someone new to the area. (It also includes a description of the other mailboxes.)

Mail box # 2 (reached by pushing the number two key on a touch-tone phone at any time) give the schedule-in-brief for about the next six weeks, including BC, OR, and other meets listed in the Bearing 315. (Richard plans to update this about twice a month.) The information given is meet location, type, start times, and contact person and phone number. (It is not possible to include driving instruction due to space and time restriction here.)

Mailbox # 3 (reached in a similar way) will have driving instructions and contact info for the next meet in the greater Seattle area. Meet directors are welcome/encouraged to also update this message directly with more information: contact Richard to get the simple procedure.

Mailbox # 4 (reached likewise) is used for upcoming major events that require pre-registration. Meet directors or registrars will be encouraged to update this as need. Please contact Richard to get the access and update procedures.

Trip Report

Florida O-THAW

by Bill Cusworth, COC

I thought I would give a report on the O-THAW A-meet that took place this past weekend in central Florida, just NW of Orlando. The weather was very similar to Seattle - cool and cloudy. Actually, it rained pretty hard the day we arrived (Friday) but the rain let up on Saturday and Sunday. The temperatures were in the 50s during the day and 40s at night. Five orienteers from Cascade OC traveled to the event - Bruce McAlister, Ken Lew, Knut Olson, Eric Bone, & myself. Many of the competitors, including Eric, Bruce, & I stayed in heated cabins located on the Day 2 map area, Wekiwa Springs State Park. We had the heater in the cabin running at maximum the whole time we were there! Ken Lew took 2nd in M65A on the Brown course while Knut Olson took 3rd. In M21A, Eric took 1st place overall while I finished 2nd in a field of 21 competitors on the Blue course.

The Day 1 area, Rock Springs Run State Reserve (RSRSR), consisted mostly of open land that contained either tall grass or palmetto. Palmetto can be likened to a cross between a fern and palm tree. The bush size can vary from 1 ft to 10 ft in height. The area also contained many marshes and depressions. A recent forest fire burned a section of the area that Blue course crossed. Eric had a great day beating the nearest competitor in M21A on the Blue course by 13 minutes while I finished 5th, 2 min. out of 2nd. Unfortunately, there was one really bad bingo control (#8) on the course which Vladimir Gusiatnikov & I both protested. The jury agreed with us that the feature (a clearing in a semi-open area!) was indistinct, that the marker was 10-15 m from the center of the circle, and that the marker was not visible since it was hung on the ground. However, the protest was denied so Vlad & I are filing a grievance with USOF.

The Day 2 area, Wekiwa Springs State Park, was much more forested than the day 1 area, and more interesting with many sinkholes and depressions and distinct palmetto thickets and palm trees growing amid the oak forest. The forest was extremely fast, the visibility was great, and there was less than 1% climb on the courses (this applied to both days). Vlad won the 9.1 km Blue course in 53:57 while I finished 2nd in 60:25. Eric had an error-filled run finishing ~6.5 min. behind me and less than a min. out of 3rd place. However, Eric still won overall as Vlad had DNF'd the first day. Medals were given to the top 3 in each category and a special Olympics-style podium with orienteering symbols on the front was built for the awards ceremony. It was very appropriate considering the Olympics are going on right now.

The event was a lot of fun and I had one of my best performances at an A-meet. I highly recommend Florida's orienteering terrain.

Presidents Column

The Start of Something Big

by Rick Hood, COC President

With your involvement, this is going to be a super year for orienteering in the Pacific Northwest and for Cascade OC, in particular, as we are hosting the United States Orienteering Federation Classic and Relay Championships this August 7th-9th. Whether you plan to participate or participate and help, this will most certainly be one of those special moments in your (and the club's) orienteering life.

Rick Breseman is doing a first class job, as Meet Director, assembling a great team of volunteers, in order to make the big event fun and exciting for both competitive and recreational orienteers alike. (Of course, Rick B would welcome your assistance; if you're interested, please give him a call at 425.483.6592.) The enclosed flyer will give you a better feel as to why we think this is a "do tell and don't miss" event. By the way, the campground at the meet site, while a bit on the rustic side, we'll be the place to be: in addition to the usual O fun, look for campground "Frisbee Golf O" and "Hula Hoop Trail O". (While the dress code for the meals, prepared on-site by Kent BBQ Verbeck, is casual, the recommend garb is a Debbie Newell designed "Snake Elum" tri-color T-shirt or a John Rogers "Green Suit".)

Rick B. has gone even farther and set things in place for a multi-day A meet in Washington State for future years involving multiple clubs. (The 1999 dates are July 21-25, as it stands now.) Wow, let the good orienteering times roll; OK, run.

Coming back to this spring, we are gearing up for the big summer events by hosting our annual newcomers' event in Woodland Park in April and having Eric Bone, USOF team member and club training chair, host a clinic or two in late

[Page 4]

spring. We also suggest that you take advantage of the B meets offered in the Cle Elum, Spokane, and Kamloops areas. Another summer effort to help maintain our positive rapport with the parks is to host a few trail work parties in nearby parks that we use for O. Of course, there are other excellent offerings that you can see by looking at the schedule page in this issue or by visiting Howard Halter's PNW O Event webpage at http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/schedule.html or simply calling the O Hotline at 206.783.3866.

Speaking of the Hotline, we're pleased to let you know the "new voice of O" is Richard Stuadt, who is taking over for Margie Bone. (Thanks for the great years of service to Margie and Bernie, the O Dog, who sometimes remarked about certain events while Margie was recording.) Richard's contact info is on the club page; if you have comments please contact him directly. Thanks again for stepping up to the mike, Richard!

Another plus is that we have most of COC's meet directors and course setters lined up for 1998. However, we are always looking to involve others; if you are interested in learning more about course setting or meet direction or just helping in one of the less central roles, please just let one of the club contacts know.

Also, please help us spread the word about O to your family, friends, and co-workers. It would be great if we could "grow the membership" this year. (Eileen Breseman is doing a top-notch job getting the word out to the newspapers. We also have some nice Newcomer's brochure to distribute thanks to a partial USOF grant.) And at the meets, if you see an unfamiliar face at a meet, please consider going up and introducing yourself and truly making them feel welcome. Collectively, this will help us "grow O".

Additionally, I have asked Mike Fritz, who has made many key contributions to COC and whose O background extends beyond COC, to head up an ad hoc committee on Strategic Planning and Financial Management. Mike will be talking with many of you so that he and his working group can suggest a course of action for COC to consider for the next several years. (In advance, thanks Mike.)

Lastly, the board of COC really wants to remind you that you are indeed the heart and soul of the club and its activities. To make it easier for us to exchange ideas and socialize outside of meet venues, we are trying to schedule every other monthly club meeting at a local restaurant on a Sunday night. Come for dinner and the informal program and feel free to stick around for the board meeting if you like. To find out more regarding any of the above, just give me or anyone listed on the club page a call.

Pt. Defiance Park - RAMBO

January 3, 1998
Tacoma, WA
Nisqually Orienteers

The Bresemans whizzed around the Point Defiance Rambo course, punching all controls and returning to the finish line 34 minutes early, thereby winning the 1998 Rambo Trophy. Following the Bresemans were Jan Urban, Eric Bone and Arne Skog who all had perfect scores. 120 competitors challenged the thick woods and chilly weather to make it the most competitive Rambo ever. High school kids from Bremerton, Oak Harbor, South Kitsap, Everett and Thomas Jefferson turned out in large numbers. Scouts from the very large Troop 212 from Gig Harbor had impressive results. Competitors came all the way from Bellingham and Vancouver, Washington to fight the brush. Congratulations to everyone!

Meet Staff: Carl Moore, Linda Moore, Ken Peters, Kathy Miner, Steve Miner, Jan Whaley, Sid Whaley, Sven Stroosma, Bert Brown.

With this meet we christened this new city-park map in downtown Richland. Turn out was good, about 20, especially considering there was limited publicity. Nevertheless, we orienteered on the black and white map version under unseasonable warm skies. Special mention goes to two quick juniors, Brendon Higham and Jon Brame, who seem to explode through like the three ounces of picric acid that was found a few miles away in Hanford the previous day. I failed to mention that this was a score-o, but to the best of my knowledge, everyone found every control.

The Newsletter of the Washington State Interscholastic Orienteering League

1997-98, Issue 3

From the WIOL Director

Dave Tallent

Credits
This has been a great WIOL Season. The weather cooperated except for the Champs. We have had great course, no courses were thrown out and no markers were missed placed. We have had record number of participants 149 with more than 80 at every meet. Together with coaches and public with had also 200 participants at every meet topping 200 at Luther Burbank!

None of this would have been possible without the many hours of work put in each and every week by local orienteers. We need to thank all the mappers, course designers and course setters, who are generally not visible at the meets. But many of the support staff you did see:

  • Bill Cusworth, for taking your money and handling registrations & database, & printing maps
  • Don Denbo, for producing all those results, & printing maps
  • Rick Hood, for the producing the newsletter and keeping all you students informed.
  • Howard Halter, for putting all that information on the Web
  • Debbie Newell, for mailing out hundreds of flyers to the schools, ordering WIOL pins & Awards, & making the award etc.
  • Bruce McAlister, the cookie man, who spends days baking hundreds of muffins, cookies and brownies which you (WIOL students) consume, gobble and swallow in just a few minutes!!! Thank you Bruce. You should also take note: Not only is Bruce a good cook, he is also a wonderful resource. He can converse on any subjects and in multiple languages and probably answer any homework questions you may have! Give him a try!

Thanks to the Field Checkers, Map Makers, Course Planners, Course Setters, Course Vetters, and Start Crews.

The finish crew: David Tallent, Debbie Newell, Jan Urban, Mark Howlett, Don Denbo and student Jenny Pearson. These people sat there for several hours each week. They check your punches, calculated your elapse time and posted your time with lightening speed. Also Randy Brassfield, Linda Richardson, Bob Reddick, Paul Magnus, Rick Breseman and many others stepped in when needed.

AND I'm sure there were many more.. Please forgive me for not remembering your names.

What will be NEW for Next Year?

  • We can expect many more students.
  • We will offer a training clinic (possible in a classroom setting) and training at the three practice meets.
  • Split HS into Varsity & Jr. Varsity so we can offer more courses
  • Beef up the Elementary & Middle School participation
  • We will try to use all challenging areas for the 6 regular meets (some like Hamlin Park)
  • And maybe we could hold the Championship meet in a "REAL" Forest.
  • We would like each school to adopt a meet and get their teachers and parents involved.

If you have any suggestions please drop me a line @ 6216 NE 154th St. Bothell WA 98011 or just call (425) 488-7471

The WIOL Post-Season

Rick Hood, WIOL Newsletter Editor

Thanks to one and all for making this one of the best WIOL seasons ever!!! Congratulations to everyone who earned a

[Page 9]

WIOL pin at the Champs or won a season or championship award. For those who are going on to college, the military, or joining the work force, all of us on the WIOL staff wish you the best. For those of you who will be in school next year, we look forward to having you be part of the 1998-99 WIOL Season.

Regardless, between now and next year there are plenty of great orienteering events and opportunities locally and around the country. One of the local orienteering clubs (Cascade or Sammamish OC, for example) usually hosts an in-town event at a local park at least once a month. If you are 18 years of age or younger, you get the best deal, which is currently a $3 youth rate entry fee.

Some big events to look forward to are Sammamish's Bullfrog "B" meets in the Cle Elum area in May. Of course, THE BIG EVENT is the US Championships to be hosted by Cascade on brand new maps in the Cle Elum area this August 7-9. Now, if you want some international O experiences, plan on making the trip up to the Sage Event the week before. (There are other events, just look at the schedule page on the inside back cover of every Bearing 315)

We also invite you to join one of the orienteering clubs in the area at the special youth rate of $5; you will then get the Bearing 315 six times a year. (Of, course your family might want to join; all the info is on the form. But if you have any questions just call one of the contact numbers listed on that page.)

Some other pretty cool stuff is also coming up: Eric Bone, COC Training Chairman, will host an O clinic before the US Champs; Rick Hood, COC Environmental Concerns Chairman, will host a few trailwork projects; and Dave Tallent, who is likely to remain on as WIOL Director, will be hosting some "WIOL in 98-99" programs. (Please see Dave's article in this issue.)

So, on behalf of everyone, thanks again for making this a super WIOL season.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Mike and Vivien Fritz will take us cross-county in their Airstream with their 8 minute video and add some commentary along the way. We will then have a "quick O tip". At 6:30 we shift to the COC board meeting proper. All are welcome to stay, but you are also most welcome to just come for the social aspects. (Detail about the trail work projects at Woodland and St. Edward State Parks and other breaking news about the US Champs are on the agenda. March 15, 1998 Ingemar Jansson Haverstad from Norway will be in Seattle in March. Ingemar was a student at the University of Washington. While here, he was a member of Cascade Orienteering Club. You may have seen his name on the University of Washington map. (At that time he was not yet a Haverstad.)

We are having a potluck for those who wish to come and say hi to Ingemar. The potluck will be at Robert & Sandy Kish Millers, 6003 Greenwood Ave. N, Seattle. Time will be 3 - 7 p.m. If you knew Ingemar or wish to meet Ingemar, please join us. Call 206-782-1437 with any questions.

I would like to add my thanks to the folks that made the WIOL school league and Winter Series possible. I will not name names for fear of leaving someone out. I just want to let the workers know that I appreciate the time commitment it took to make these events successes. Sandy Kish Miller

COC will be hosting a trail project in Woodland Park this late April or early May. We will have all the details on the Howard's PNW website in short order. The club will also return to St. Ed later in the season for another project. Of course, anyone is welcome to pitch in; matter-of-fact, we would really like your help in staying in good graces with the land mangers and the public.

Woodland Park staff are really excited about us coming and helping out with some much needed trail work. This sounds like a truly win-win situation for us and them. Hope you will consider joining us for the day in late April or early May. For more info, please call Rick H at 206.367.2606.
Eileen Breseman, COC Publicity and Promotion
New connections for publicity are widening our exposure to the public.

On the Internet: We have our event calendar listed, a writeup about what orienteering is all about, as well as a direct connection to the Pacific NW Orienteering Website at two new sites. Try these: www.ontherun.com a runner's guide to what's hap-

pening in the Northwest; www.sports-etc.com a monthly multi-sport magazine in its 10th year; http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html our own Pacific NW O website created and continually updated by Howard Halter, webmaster extraordinaire. Great information and a link to the US Championships coming in August with on-line entry application.
Radio: KMTT The Mountain station deejay offered to make public service announcements about us during his shift between 6-10pm Monday through Friday.

Newspaper: For those who did not get the word, The Seattle Times covered us both at St. Edward State Park in December, and at Lynndale Park on the trim course with member Jim Siscel, and gave us a really nice writeup and large photo in the January 9th pullout, Weekend Happenings (centerfold!) of The Seattle Times.

Future plans: We plan to make an easy set-up informational reader board at meets for onsite education, publicity, and information. We hope also to connect with local neighborhood papers to include upcoming meets in their area. If you have additional ideas they would be welcomed. Contact Eileen Breseman breseman@msn.com or (425) 483-6592. Publicity gets the people out to our meets!

It would be very appreciated if you - yes, you - took a few moments to give these supportive media people some positive feedback on their coverage of our orienteering doings. It goes a long way on the goodwill-o-meter.

SACO land is Southeastern Washington, i.e, Tri-Cities and Walla Walla. You may have heard of our free Score-O meet on January 31 at Howard Amon in Richland (results elsewhere in this issue) followed by our annual meeting. But there has been a few more developments this year.

On Feb. 13, I held a seminar for 29 at the YMCA in Walla Walla. I gave a short introduction, showed a video, and made a little meet in the big gym on a map that I made the day before. YMCA Program Director Tom Kelleher remarked, "Cooooooool!" I do not think this will be the last time I have heard from Tom.

We have also moved on with some mapping projects. Ivar completed the first OCAD draft of my entire Pioneer Park (Walla Walla) field notes and I have already sent him back some corrections. Hey, we are amateurs at this so it will take a few passes.

Ivar and I also spent a day at Camp Wooten up on the Tucannon River east of Dayton. We used a laser range finder, compasses, and paces to get data for a base map of the area. Our motivation? I have held seminars for over 300 kids there on a primitive park map. I will be having contact with a few hundred more this year.
Our Spring schedule is as follows
Sun, Mar 29 Sacajawea; Sat, Apr 11 Bennington; Sun, Apr 26 Horn Rapids; Sun, May 10 Rooks Park; and Sat, May 23 Wallula Junction

This was an exceptionally active year. 417 people had contact with the club, through a combination of eight regular events (148 participants) and ten special sessions (269 participants) for Boy Scout troops, church groups, a local college, and grade schools.

Three new areas were mapped this year. Pioneer Park West (1:3000 scale. 0.1 sq km) was mapped in the city of Walla Walla and Howard Amon Park (1:4000 scale, 0.15 sq km) in the city of Richland. The small maps, in populated areas will facilitate our teaching of young people. The third area mapped is near Wallula, WA, at the confluence of the Columbia and Walla Walla Rivers (15 miles south of the confluence of the Columbia and Snake Rivers). Two maps have been created from this area. The Madame Dorion map (1:5000, 0.3 sq km) centers about a park with facilities, and is suitable for beginner and perhaps some intermediate courses. This map is contained within the much larger Wallula Junction map (1:15000, 10 sq km), which is fully 7 km from one end to the other.
The club gratefully acknowledges their first corporate sponsor, Sporthaus, which donated the use of a $250 laser range finder for map making.

I thought I would give a report on the O-THAW A-meet that took place this past weekend in central Florida, just NW of Orlando. The weather was very similar to Seattle - cool and cloudy. Actually, it rained pretty hard the day we arrived (Friday) but the rain let up on Saturday and Sunday. The temperatures were in the 50s during the day and 40s at night. Five orienteers from Cascade OC traveled to the event - Bruce McAlister, Ken Lew, Knut Olson, Eric Bone, & myself. Many of the competitors, including Eric, Bruce, & I stayed in heated cabins located on the Day 2 map area, Wekiwa Springs State Park. We had the heater in the cabin running at maximum the whole time we were there! Ken Lew took 2nd in M65A on the Brown course while Knut Olson took 3rd. In M21A, Eric took 1st place overall while I finished 2nd in a field of 21 competitors on the Blue course.

The Day 1 area, Rock Springs Run State Reserve (RSRSR), consisted mostly of open land that contained either tall grass or palmetto. Palmetto can be likened to a cross between a fern and palm tree. The bush size can vary from 1 ft to 10 ft in height. The area also contained many marshes and depressions. A recent forest fire burned a section of the area that Blue course crossed. Eric had a great day beating the nearest competitor in M21A on the Blue course by 13 minutes while I finished 5th, 2 min. out of 2nd. Unfortunately, there was one really bad bingo control (#8) on the course which Vladimir Gusiatnikov & I both protested. The jury agreed with us that the feature (a clearing in a semi-open area!) was indistinct, that the marker was 10-15 m from the center of the circle, and that the marker was not visible since it was hung on the ground. However, the protest was denied so Vlad & I are filing a grievance with USOF.

The Day 2 area, Wekiwa Springs State Park, was much more forested than the day 1 area, and more interesting with many sinkholes and depressions and distinct palmetto thickets and palm trees growing amid the oak forest. The forest was extremely fast, the visibility was great, and there was less than 1% climb on the courses (this applied to both days). Vlad won the 9.1 km Blue course in 53:57 while I finished 2nd in 60:25. Eric had an error-filled run finishing ~6.5 min. behind me and less than a min. out of 3rd place. However, Eric still won overall as Vlad had DNF'd the first day. Medals were given to the top 3 in each category and a special Olympics-style podium with orienteering symbols on the front was built for the awards ceremony. It was very appropriate considering the Olympics are going on right now.
The event was a lot of fun and I had one of my best performances at an A-meet. I highly recommend Florida's orienteering terrain.

With your involvement, this is going to be a super year for orienteering in the Pacific Northwest and for Cascade OC, in particular, as we are hosting the United States Orienteering Federation Classic and Relay Championships this August 7th-9th. Whether you plan to participate or participate and help, this will most certainly be one of those special moments in your (and the club's) orienteering life.

Rick Breseman is doing a first class job, as Meet Director, assembling a great team of volunteers, in order to make the big event fun and exciting for both competitive and recreational orienteers alike. (Of course, Rick B would welcome your assistance; if you're interested, please give him a call at 425.483.6592.) The enclosed flyer will give you a better feel as to why we think this is a "do tell and don't miss" event. By the way, the campground at the meet site, while a bit on the rustic side, we'll be the place to be: in addition to the usual O fun, look for campground "Frisbee Golf O" and "Hula Hoop Trail O". (While the dress code for the meals, prepared on-site by Kent BBQ Verbeck, is casual, the recommend garb is a Debbie Newell designed "Snake Elum" tri-color T-shirt or a John Rogers "Green Suit".)

Rick B. has gone even farther and set things in place for a multi-day A meet in Washington State for future years involving multiple clubs. (The 1999 dates are July 21-25, as it stands now.) Wow, let the good orienteering times roll; OK, run.

Coming back to this spring, we are gearing up for the big summer events by hosting our annual newcomers' event in Woodland Park in April and having Eric Bone, USOF team member and club training chair, host a clinic or two in late

spring. We also suggest that you take advantage of the B meets offered in the Cle Elum, Spokane, and Kamloops areas. Another summer effort to help maintain our positive rapport with the parks is to host a few trail work parties in nearby parks that we use for O. Of course, there are other excellent offerings that you can see by looking at the schedule page in this issue or by visiting Howard Halter's PNW O Event webpage at http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/schedule.html or simply calling the O Hotline at 206.783.3866.

Speaking of the Hotline, we're pleased to let you know the "new voice of O" is Richard Stuadt, who is taking over for Margie Bone. (Thanks for the great years of service to Margie and Bernie, the O Dog, who sometimes remarked about certain events while Margie was recording.) Richard's contact info is on the club page; if you have comments please contact him directly. Thanks again for stepping up to the mike, Richard!

Another plus is that we have most of COC's meet directors and course setters lined up for 1998. However, we are always looking to involve others; if you are interested in learning more about course setting or meet direction or just helping in one of the less central roles, please just let one of the club contacts know.

Also, please help us spread the word about O to your family, friends, and co-workers. It would be great if we could "grow the membership" this year. (Eileen Breseman is doing a top-notch job getting the word out to the newspapers. We also have some nice Newcomer's brochure to distribute thanks to a partial USOF grant.) And at the meets, if you see an unfamiliar face at a meet, please consider going up and introducing yourself and truly making them feel welcome. Collectively, this will help us "grow O".

Additionally, I have asked Mike Fritz, who has made many key contributions to COC and whose O background extends beyond COC, to head up an ad hoc committee on Strategic Planning and Financial Management. Mike will be talking with many of you so that he and his working group can suggest a course of action for COC to consider for the next several years. (In advance, thanks Mike.)

Lastly, the board of COC really wants to remind you that you are indeed the heart and soul of the club and its activities. To make it easier for us to exchange ideas and socialize outside of meet venues, we are trying to schedule every other monthly club meeting at a local restaurant on a Sunday night. Come for dinner and the informal program and feel free to stick around for the board meeting if you like. To find out more regarding any of the above, just give me or anyone listed on the club page a call.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
